coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodots for ultrasensitive fluorescent detection of aqueous acetone based on synergistic electron and energy transfer
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Sai, Liman
Ding, Meijuan
Zhou, Xinge
Chang, Quanhong
Huang, Lei
coredata.dc:description: Sensing of acetone in aqueous solution is important for both environmental and medical applications. However, current probes suffer from low sensitivity and a slow response. In this work, carbon nanodots (CNDs) derived from β-Lactoglobulin were developed for highly sensitive detection of aqueous acetone. The CNDs possessed both spectral matching and surface recognition sites toward acetone. Selective fluorescent quenching of the CNDs by acetone was realized via inner filter effect. Moreover, photoinduced electron transfer from the CNDs to acetone further contributed to the suppressing of the fluorescent signal. The synergistic effect of electron and energy transfer resulted in high sensitivity of the CNDs probe, the limit of detection reached as low as 0.5 μM. The unique ultraviolet emission and tunable surface amino groups of the CNDs were responsible for their superior sensing performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Fluorescent probe
Synergistic effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of acetone in aqueous solution
Structure and morphology characterization of the CNDs
Optimize of the excitation wavelength in the IFE process
Enhancement of the quenching efficiency via PET
Optimizing of the PET process through modulation of the surface groups by EDA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) TEM and (c, d) HRTEM images of the CNDs. Inset: size distribution histogram of the nanodots in Fig. 1a and b.
(a) FTIR spectrum of the CNDs. (b) XPS survey spectrum of the CNDs and high-resolution scans of (c) C1s and (d) N1s spectra of the CNDs.

(a) UV–vis spectrum of the CNDs. (b) PL spectrum of the CNDs excited at 270 nm. (c) PLE spectrum of the CNDs recorded at 365 nm (black curve) and absorption spectrum of acetone (red curve). (d) Scheme of the synthesis of the CNDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescent quenching of the CNDs by increasing amount of acetone. (b) Stern-Volmer plot of the fluorescent quenching of the CNDs by acetone over the range of 0.6-7.8 μL (in 3 mL mixed solution). (c) UV–vis spectra of acetone and other organic analytes. (d) Selectivity of the CNDs for acetone over other organic interferences. The intensity indicated the PL of the 365 nm peak (with 270 nm excitation) of the CNDs in the presence of 10 μL analytes (the total volume of the mixed solution was 3 mL).
PL spectra of the CNDs with different excitation wavelength (a) in the absence and (b) in the presence of 10 μL acetone. The mixed solution was 3 mL.

Time-resolved PL decay curves of the different emission peaks of the CNDs in the absence and presence of acetone. The wavelength of the emission peaks were shown in each picture. The insert pictures showed the fluorescent intensity of the corresponding emission peaks without acetone (black curve) and with 10 μL acetone (red curve) in 3 mL solution. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Time-resolved PL decay curves of the CNDs synthesized with different amount of EDA in the absence and presence of acetone. The decay was recorded at 365 nm emission.
Hydrogen bond between acetone and surface amino groups of the CNDs.

Fluorescent quenching efficiency (I/I0) of the CNDs by acetone with different excitation wavelength. I and I0 represent the fluorescent intensity of the CNDs with and without acetone, respectively. Noted that the emission wavelength of the CNDs varied with the excitation wavelength. Therefore, the fluorescence was recorded at 390 nm with excitation from 250-330 nm, and at the corresponding emission peak wavelength with excitation from 340-360 nm.
Fluorescent quenching efficiency (I/I0) of the 365 nm peak of the CNDs by acetone. The CNDs were synthesize with different amount of EDA.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots for ultrasensitive fluorescent detection of aqueous acetone based on synergistic electron and energy transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Shanghai Normal University, Guilin Road 100, Shanghai 200234, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Shanghai Normal University, Guilin Road 100, Shanghai 200234, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering and College of Chemistry & Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering and College of Chemistry & Molecular Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering and College of Chemistry & Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensing of acetone in aqueous solution is important for both environmental and medical applications. However, current probes suffer from low sensitivity and a slow response. In this work, carbon nanodots (CNDs) derived from β-Lactoglobulin were developed for highly sensitive detection of aqueous acetone. The CNDs possessed both spectral matching and surface recognition sites toward acetone. Selective fluorescent quenching of the CNDs by acetone was realized via inner filter effect. Moreover, photoinduced electron transfer from the CNDs to acetone further contributed to the suppressing of the fluorescent signal. The synergistic effect of electron and energy transfer resulted in high sensitivity of the CNDs probe, the limit of detection reached as low as 0.5 μM. The unique ultraviolet emission and tunable surface amino groups of the CNDs were responsible for their superior sensing performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Acetone is widely used in cosmetics and adhesives and miscible with water, thus can be easily found in the environment due to sewage disposal or industrial leaching [1]. In surface waters, acetone can be concentrated due to photodegradation of dissolved organic matter or microbial processes. High content of acetone in water has potential toxic effects to human body. It may cause skin dryness, throat, eye irritation or dermatitis [2]. Due to its adverse health effects, detection of acetone in aqueous solution has attracted great interests. Moreover, in the field of pathological analysis, acetone in urine or blood can be used as a potential biomarker for disease diagnosis such as diabetes mellitus or starving [3,4]. Therefore, acetone sensing in aqueous solution is important for both environmental and medical applications.

Traditional methods for aqueous acetone sensing were mainly chromatographic techniques [5,6], which required complex facilities and pre-treatment of the samples. In recent years, fluorescent sensing of acetone has been realized using metal-organic framework (MOF) [7–9] or coordination polymers (CPs) [10,11]. These fluorescent probes showed advantage of simple manipulation and high repeatability. However, the sophisticated synthetic methods of the MOFs limited their application. Besides, the separated binding sites in the complex structure of the MOFs prolonged the response time.

As a rising fluorescent material, carbon nanodots (CNDs) have attracted great interests due to their superiority of high fluorescence, good photostability and low toxicity [12–14]. In the sensing field, they have been used in the oxide nanocomposites for improving the acetone gas sensing property [15–17]. Recently, the CNDs were also directly used as a fluorescent probe toward acetone with various sensing mechanism. Das et al. reported acetone sensing by N, S-doped CNDs, the fluorescent quenching was attributed to the interaction of acetone with surface groups of the CNDs [18]. B-doped CNDs [19] and biomass-derived CNDs [20] were also used as polarity sensors for acetone detection in solution and vapor phase. In our previous report [21], CNDs derived from glucose were used for aqueous acetone sensing via inner filter effect (IFE) [22]. However, further improvement of the sensitivity is difficult, and it is still desirable to develop new-generation of acetone probes.

Here, we use CNDs derived from β-Lactoglobulin (LG) for acetone sensing in aqueous solution. LG is a high hydrophilic protein which has been used as a capping agent for synthesis of the quantum dots [23]. Having long carbon chains, it can also serve as a carbon source for construction of the CNDs. Various kinds of element doping in the synthetic process can be expected owing to the abundant chemical groups possessed by LG. The LG-derived CNDs had large spectral overlap with acetone in the ultraviolet region, which ensured efficient fluorescent quenching of the CNDs by acetone via IFE. Moreover, ethylenediamine (EDA) was used in the synthesis for tuning of the surface groups of the CNDs. The amino groups on the surface of the CNDs could interact with acetone, and allowed photoinduced electron transfer (PET) to occur from the CNDs to acetone. The synergistic combination of the IFE and PET led to significant quenching of the fluorescent signal. Therefore, high sensitivity of the CNDs toward acetone was achieved, which made them a superior probe for acetone sensing in aqueous solution.

β-Lactoglobulin (LG, molecular weight～18 kDa) was purchased from Sigma (USA). Ethylenediamine (EDA) was purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagents Company (China). Milli-Q water was used as the solvent for all solutions. All the reagents were used without further purification.

CNDs were derived from LG protein according to the previous report by our research group [24]. In brief, 100 mg of LG was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water. A certain amount of EDA (0−600 μL) was added to the LG solution under ultrasonication. In case of no EDA, the pH value of the LG solution was adjusted to 11 by NaOH. The mixed solution was transferred to a stainless-steel Teflon lined vessel, followed by hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 7 h. Purification was done by dialysis (0.5 KD) for two days in 1000 mL deionized water, and the obtained CNDs solution was stored at 4 °C.

The PL quantum yield of the CNDs was estimated using quinine sulfate (Φ = 54 % in 0.1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution) as the fluorescence standard. The quantum yield was calculated according to the following equation:
(1) ΦX = ΦS(ASIX/AXIS)(nX/nS)2
Where Φ is the quantum yield; I is the integrated PL intensity; A is the absorbance value at the excitation wavelength; n is the refractive index of the solvents used. Subscripts S and X represent the standard substance and the test sample, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of acetone in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: For acetone sensing, a certain amount of acetone was mixed with diluted CNDs solution, then deionized water was added to the mixed solution to reach the total amount of 3 mL. Fluorescent intensity of the CNDs with different amount of acetone was measured after 10 s at room temperature. To investigate the influence of different analytes, acetone was replaced with acetic acid, 2-butanone, DMSO, acetonitrile, DMF, methanol, ethanol, isopropanol, formaldehyde and acetaldehyde. Typically, 10 μL of the above organic solvents was freshly taken and added to the 3 mL CNDs solution. Fluorescent quenching of the CNDs by different analytes was measured.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were recorded on a JEOL-2011 electron microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 KV. Samples for TEM and HRTEM were prepared by dropping CNDs solution onto a carbon-coated copper grid. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained using a Philips X'Pert diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a NEXUS-870 spectrophotometer (frequency range from 4000 to 400 cm−1). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was measured on a VG ESCALAB 220i-XL surface analysis system. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a Lambda 950 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer). Photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu RF-6310PC spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Columbia, MD, USA). The fluorescent lifetimes of the CNDs were measured by using a single-photon counting spectrometer using an Edinburgh FLS980 spectrometer equipped with a continuous Xe900 xenon lamp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and morphology characterization of the CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: For morphology characterization of the LG-CNDs, TEM and HRTEM images were recorded and showed in Fig. 1 . In the TEM images (Fig. 1a and b), the nanodots showed good monodispersity with a spherical shape. The sizes of the CNDs were in the range of 3−10 nm. The inhomogeneous polycondensation of the protein might be responsible for the wide size distribution. Fig. 1c and d shows an individual nanodot of 7 nm and 3 nm, respectively. The well-resolved lattice fringe in HRTEM images indicated the sp2 domains existing in the CNDs. The crystal lattice was of ca. 0.21 nm in Fig. 1c, which could be attributed to the (100) lattice plane of graphene [25]. Lattice fringe with different direction could also be found in a single nanodot (Fig. 1d), indicating the CNDs were composed of small sp2 fractions. The Raman spectrum of the CNDs is shown in Figure S1. The peak at 1587 cm−1 (G band) indicated E2g mode of the graphite structure, which was related to sp2 carbon in the hexagonal lattice [26].

The FTIR and XPS spectroscopy were used to investigate the chemical composition and surface groups of the CNDs. In the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2 a), the absorption peaks at 3414 cm−1 and 1380 cm−1 corresponded to OH and −CH2- groups, respectively. Peaks at 1635 cm−1, 1576 cm−1 and 1487 cm−1 could be assigned to the amide I CO [27], amide II NH [28] and amide III CN [29] stretching vibration, respectively. These peaks indicated formation of the amide bonds during dehydration process in the synthesis of CNDs. The XPS survey spectrum in Fig. 2b shows the elemental compositions in CNDs of C, N and O. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) revealed CC/CC groups (284.7 eV), CO/CN groups (286.0 eV) and CO groups (287.6 eV) [30]. The N1s spectrum in Fig. 2d revealed two types of N doping: pyrrolic N (399.6 eV) and graphitic N (400.1 eV) [31].

The UV–vis, PL and PLE spectra were recorded to characterize the optical property of the CNDs. The CNDs synthesized with 300 μL of EDA were taken as an example. There were three recognized peaks in the absorption spectrum (Fig. 3 a): 280 nm, 287 nm and 320 nm. According to previous reports, the 280 and 287 nm absorption peaks should be assigned to n-π* transition of CO or CO bonds [32], while the 320 nm absorption peak belonged to the π-π* transition in an extended conjugation system or surface states [26]. The PL spectrum of the CNDs excited with 270 nm showed multi-band emission in the ultraviolet region, which was rarely found in CNDs (Fig. 3b). The short-wavelength emission of the CNDs also resulted in blue shift of their PLE spectra. As shown in Fig. 3c, the large spectral overlap between CNDs and acetone could facilitate efficient energy transfer. Here, the unique optical property of the CNDs made them a good a candidate for fluorescent acetone sensing via IFE.

For detailed investigation of the optical property of the CNDs, PL spectra with the excitation ranging from 250 to 390 nm were recorded and shown in Figure S2a. With low-energy excitation from 340 to 390 nm, emission peaks of surface states could be found, which red-shifted with the increase of the excitation wavelength. These surface-state related emission were usually induced by chemical groups [28,33]. A photograph of the CNDs solution with 430 nm emission (excited with 365 nm light) is shown in Figure S2a, inset. With high-energy excitation in the range of 250−290 nm, the CNDs showed multi-band emission with three overlapping peaks: 302 nm, 365 nm and 390 nm. The PLE spectra of the three peaks are shown in Figure S2b. The 280, 285 and 320 nm PLE peaks consisted well with the 280, 287 and 320 nm peaks in the absorption spectrum (Fig. 3a), indicating the emissions were from radiative transition of π electrons. The three PL peaks with different wavelength indicated different π-π* energy gaps. Here, the CNDs were derived from protein, which had long carbon chain functionalized with abundant chemical groups. Intermolecular dehydration occurred between the peptide chains of the protein, and aromatic structure was formed (Fig. 3d). In this condensation process, various kinds of element doping could be expected. It has been reported that, hybridization of N [34] or O [35] atoms into the aromatic sp2 structure resulted in widen of the energy gap and blue shift of the emission wavelength. Here, the ultraviolet PL of the CNDs might result from different kinds of element doping in the sp2 domains. The PL decay curves of the three emission peaks were also measured (Figure S2c, fitted lifetime components showed in Table S1). The data showed similar fluorescent decay between the three peaks, indicating they were originated from same basic structures. A brief scheme of the energy structure of the CNDs is shown in Figure S2d. The quantum yield of the CNDs excited at 300 was estimated to be 12 %.

The photostability of the LG-CNDs was tested under continuous UV illumination. As shown in Figure S3a, with UV irradiation for 80 min (365 nm, 16 W), the PL spectra of the CNDs did not show significant change, indicating their good photostability. The fluorescence of the CNDs in the presence of various metal ions (30 μM) was also measured. As shown in Figure S3b, Cu2+ had an obvious quenching effect on the fluorescence of the LG-CNDs. This might be due to the chelation interaction between Cu2+ and surface amino groups of the CNDs, which was also demonstrated in our previous work [24]. Besides Cu2+, the PL of the CNDs showed good stability in the presence of common ions existing in the environment, which makes them suitable for practical application.

The CNDs with unique optical property were applied for sensing of acetone in aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence of the CNDs could be gradually quenched with increasing amount of acetone. The relationship between the quenching efficiency (I/I0) and volume of acetone (in 3 mL mixed solution) is shown in Fig. 4b. At low content range of acetone, the relationship between I/I0 and concentration of acetone could be described by the Stern-Volmer equation:
(2) I/I0 = 1 + Ks[c]
Where Ks is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, I and I0 represent the fluorescent intensity of the CNDs with and without acetone, respectively. A linear correlation (R2 = 0.997) was found between the fluorescence response of CNDs and the acetone content within the range of 3.44 mM–19.8 mM. The detection limit (3σ/K) was determined to be 0.5 μM, which showed ultrasensitive response of the CNDs probe toward acetone. A comparison of the recently reported acetone sensing probes with the CNDs probe here is shown in Table S2.

Besides the sensitivity, high selectivity is also important in a sensing system. Here, selectivity of the CNDs probe was realized taking advantage of the intrinsic optical property of acetone. Fig. 4c shows the UV–vis spectra of acetone and other organic matters. The absorption band of acetone extended to 320 nm, which differed it from other interferences. The excitation light of the CNDs could only be largely absorbed by acetone, but not other organic molecules (Fig. 3c). Therefore, high selectivity of the CNDs probe toward acetone was ensured among the common organic interferences (Fig. 4d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimize of the excitation wavelength in the IFE process
ce:section.ce:para: Here, the large spectral overlap between CNDs and acetone provided chance for design of an IFE-based sensing system. As the influence of the excitation light played a key role, fluorescent quenching efficiency was compared in case of different excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. 5 and Table 1 , the quenching efficiency (I/I0) of the CNDs by 10 μL acetone reached the lowest value at the excitation wavelength of 270 nm. This was due to highest absorption coefficient of acetone at 270 nm (Fig. 3c), which led to greatest absorption of the excitation light, and subsequent suppressing of the fluorescent signal. The data also provided evidence that the IFE process contributed to the fluorescent quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement of the quenching efficiency via PET
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, the absorption spectrum of acetone extended to 320 nm. Therefore, the excitation light at the wavelength longer than 320 could hardly be absorbed by acetone. However, there was still considerable fluorescent quenching of the CNDs in the excitation range from 330 to 360 nm. This suggested another quenching process functioning in the sensing system. To investigate the electronic behavior of the CNDs in detail, fluorescent lifetimes of the CNDs in the presence and absence of acetone were measured, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6 , there was obvious decrease of the lifetime of the CNDs with adding of acetone (fitted components listed in Table S3), indicating strong interaction between CNDs and acetone in the excited state. For PL peaks with different emission wavelength, the decrease of the lifetimes showed different extent with same amount of acetone. Moreover, the quenching efficiency (I/I0) of the emission peaks was directly related to the changing of the lifetime. With the same excitation wavelength of 270 nm (Fig. 6a and b) when the influence of IFE should be the same, the quenching of the fluorescence on 365 nm peak was greater than that of the 390 nm peak. Meanwhile, decrease of the lifetime of the 365 nm peak was also greater. With the excitation wavelength beyond 320 nm when there was no IFE, fluorescent quenching by acetone still occurred in the surface-state emission peaks (Fig. 6c and d). Quenching of the 400 nm peak was greater than that of the 415 nm peak, accompanied with greater decrease of the lifetime.

The data clearly indicated influence of the excited electrons in the CNDs by acetone, and direct relationship between the lifetime decreasing and fluorescent quenching. It has been reported that, CNDs could be both good electron donor or electron acceptor [36]. Photoinduced electron transfer between CNDs and other small molecules was commonly reported [37–39]. Here, the target molecule of acetone had electron-withdrawing carbonyl groups, which could form hydrogen bond with electron-donating amino groups on the surface of the CNDs (Scheme 1 ). Therefore, electron transfer from CNDs to acetone was likely to occur. Previous report showed that, the driving force in the PET process could be influenced by the differential between the excited states of the donor and acceptor. Excited electrons in different energy levels had different transfer rate [40]. Here, the fluorescent quenching of the 365 nm peak by acetone was the greatest, which might be due to fast transfer rate of the electron in the elevated excited state of this ultraviolet emission peak.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing of the PET process through modulation of the surface groups by EDA
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, PET process requires molecular interaction between the donor and acceptor. Here, the surface amino groups of the CNDs was believed to be responsible for the chemical bonding with acetone. It has been reported in our previous work that, LG-CNDs with more surface amino groups could be obtained by increasing amount of EDA used in the synthetic process [24]. Figure S4 showed the PL spectra of the CNDs synthesized with different amount of EDA. The CNDs showed a dominant 390 nm peak as well as suppressed surface-state emission with increasing of the EDA. This was due to higher N doping into the sp2 aromatic structures with participation of EDA in the dehydration process. Most importantly, the consuming of the carboxyl groups in the protein by EDA could result in more amino groups covered on the obtained CNDs (detailed discussion see our previous report in Ref. [24]. Here, modulation of the surface groups could be realized without shifting of the emission peak position of the CNDs. Therefore, the PET process could be further optimized through adjusting of the amino groups, which acted as the recognition sites for acetone. The lifetime changing as well as the quenching efficiency of the CNDs prepared with different amount of EDA are shown in Fig. 7 and Table 2 . As seen in the PL decay curves, decrease of the lifetime of the CNDs gradually became greater with the increase of EDA. This could be explained by more amino groups covered on the surface of the CNDs, which allowed bonding with more acetone molecules. Thus facilitating of the PET process improved the quenching efficiency (Table 2). However, quenching in the CNDs synthesized with 600 μL EDA became weak again, which might be due to non-radiative defects caused by excess amino groups. The data showed that the optimized amount of EDA should be 300 μL.

In this work, a high sensitive fluorescent probe based on LG protein-derived CNDs was developed for acetone sensing in aqueous solution. Owing to spectral overlap between the CNDs and acetone in the ultraviolet region, the fluorescence of the CNDs could be selectively quenched by acetone via energy transfer. Moreover, electron transfer from the excited state of the CNDs to acetone further suppressed the fluorescent signal. As a result, the CNDs could function as a high sensitive probe for aqueous acetone sensing, and also provided chances for developing acetone vapor sensor in the future.

Liman Sai conducted the experiments, analyzed experimental results and wrote the manuscript. Meijuan Ding did the fluorescent lifetime measurements of the CNDs. Xinge Zhou assisted in sensing experiments of various organic molecules. Quanhong Chang assisted in reviewing the manuscript. Lei Huang conceived the idea and supervised the project. All authors read and contributed to the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All the authors are aware of the submission and agree to its publication. We confirm that the present submission is original and not under consideration for publication elsewhere. There is no conflict of interest with other people or organization.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (No. 18070502800), the Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai (No. 16ZR1424400), Shanghai Municipal Education Committee Key Laboratory of Molecular Imaging Probes and Sensors for Shanghai Universities.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2020.124677.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
